tagline: A rare orange-husked coconut variety, prized for its extra-sweet water.
description: Unlike the common green or yellow coconut, the King Coconut is a distinct cultivar known for its bright orange husk and naturally sweet, low-fat water. It is grown mainly for drinking rather than for copra or oil, and is considered a specialty variety in India.
funFacts: The orange colour comes from natural pigments in the husk, not ripeness.
King Coconut water has slightly more sugar and electrolytes than regular coconut water.
A single healthy palm can produce 60-100 fruits a year.
Coconut palms can live and bear fruit for over 60 years.

tagline: Known as the 'village pharmacy' for its powerful medicinal properties.
description: The neem tree is native to the Indian subcontinent and has been used in traditional medicine (Ayurveda) for thousands of years. Nearly every part of the tree - leaves, bark, seeds and oil - has a traditional or modern use, from skincare to natural pest control.
funFacts: Neem oil is a natural, biodegradable pesticide used in organic farming.
Neem twigs have traditionally been used as toothbrushes (datun).
A mature neem tree can absorb a significant amount of CO2 and air pollutants.
Neem trees can survive in poor soil and drought conditions.

tagline: India's National Tree - a single tree can grow into an entire grove.
description: The Banyan is famous for its aerial prop roots, which grow down from its branches and thicken into new trunks over time, allowing a single tree to spread across a huge area. It is considered sacred in Indian culture and is a symbol of longevity and shelter.
funFacts: The Great Banyan in Kolkata's botanical garden covers over 14,000 square metres.
Banyan trees are 'strangler figs' - they often start life growing on another host tree.
The tree can live for hundreds of years.
Its dense canopy supports birds, insects, and small mammals as habitat.
uses: Bark and leaves used in traditional medicine
location: Central courtyard, near the assembly ground

tagline: A sacred tree with clusters of fragrant orange-red flowers.
description: The Ashoka tree holds deep cultural and religious significance in India and is increasingly rare in the wild, even though it is commonly planted.
funFacts: Flowers open yellow and turn orange-red as they age
